findings.title: tar: Multiple Path Traversal / Arbitrary File Creation/Overwrite Vulnerabilities in sqlite3 Build Chain
findings.cwe_name: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')
findings.usage_in_codebase: tar is used exclusively by node-gyp at npm install time to extract pre-built sqlite3 native binary archives. It is never required or invoked in application runtime code (app.js does not import tar). Exploitation of these CVEs would require a maliciously crafted tar archive to be processed during sqlite3's native module installation — a supply-chain attack vector, not a runtime one.
findings.description: Three GHSA advisories (GHSA-34x7-hfp2-rc4v / CVE-2021-37712, GHSA-8qq5-rm4j-mr97 / CVE-2021-37701, GHSA-83g3-92jg-28cx / CVE-2021-32803) document insufficient symlink and path sanitization in tar, allowing attackers who control a crafted tar archive to perform arbitrary file creation, overwrite, or symbolic-link injection on the extracting machine. npm audit flags the installed tar version (6.2.1) within the sqlite3 5.x dependency tree as requiring a sqlite3 major-version upgrade to fully resolve.
findings.remediation: Upgrade sqlite3 to >=6.0.1 (`npm install sqlite3@^6.0.1`). This is a semver-major bump that refreshes the entire native build toolchain. As a short-term mitigation you may add an npm `overrides` block in package.json: `"overrides": {"tar": ">=6.2.1"}` but the canonical fix is the sqlite3 upgrade.

findings.title: cacache: Indirect High-Severity Exposure via Vulnerable tar in sqlite3 Build Chain
findings.cwe_name: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')
findings.usage_in_codebase: cacache is the content-addressable cache layer used by node-gyp's fetch pipeline (make-fetch-happen) when downloading pre-built sqlite3 binaries during npm install. It is not imported or reachable at application runtime.
findings.description: cacache 15.3.0 depends on the vulnerable tar versions that carry GHSA-34x7-hfp2-rc4v, GHSA-8qq5-rm4j-mr97, and GHSA-83g3-92jg-28cx. It inherits the high severity rating because it uses tar for content extraction in its caching operations.
findings.remediation: Upgrade sqlite3 to >=6.0.1, which depends on a newer cacache version with a non-vulnerable tar dependency.

findings.title: @tootallnate/once: Deprecated Package with Known Advisory in sqlite3 Build Chain
findings.cwe_name: Use of Unmaintained Third Party Components
findings.usage_in_codebase: @tootallnate/once is a build-time-only transitive dependency pulled in by http-proxy-agent, which is part of node-gyp's HTTP fetch machinery used during sqlite3 native binary downloads. It is never referenced in application runtime code.
findings.description: Advisory GHSA-vpq2-c234-7xj6 flags @tootallnate/once 1.1.2. The package is deprecated and has been superseded by more maintained event-once implementations. The advisory is low severity and the exposure is limited entirely to the npm install phase.
findings.remediation: Upgrade sqlite3 to >=6.0.1, which eliminates the dependency on @tootallnate/once entirely in the updated node-gyp build chain.

findings.title: http-proxy-agent: Indirect Low-Severity Exposure via @tootallnate/once Advisory
findings.cwe_name: Use of Unmaintained Third Party Components
findings.usage_in_codebase: http-proxy-agent 4.0.1 is part of node-gyp's HTTP proxy support layer used during pre-built binary downloads at npm install time. It is not imported or active in the Express application at runtime.
findings.description: http-proxy-agent 4.0.1 pulls in @tootallnate/once 1.1.2, which carries advisory GHSA-vpq2-c234-7xj6. The vulnerability is inherited through the dependency chain and is confined to the sqlite3 native module build process.
findings.remediation: Upgrade sqlite3 to >=6.0.1, which upgrades http-proxy-agent to a version that no longer depends on @tootallnate/once.

findings.title: make-fetch-happen: Aggregated High-Severity Exposure via cacache and http-proxy-agent
findings.cwe_name: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')
findings.usage_in_codebase: make-fetch-happen 9.1.0 is the HTTP fetch/retry library used by node-gyp to download pre-built sqlite3 native binaries during `npm install`. It aggregates the high-severity tar CVEs through its cacache dependency and the low-severity @tootallnate/once advisory through http-proxy-agent. Not reachable at application runtime.
findings.description: make-fetch-happen 9.1.0 inherits high severity from its dependency on cacache 15.3.0 (which depends on vulnerable tar) and low severity from http-proxy-agent (which depends on the deprecated @tootallnate/once 1.1.2). Both chains originate from the sqlite3 native build toolchain.
findings.remediation: Upgrade sqlite3 to >=6.0.1, which depends on make-fetch-happen >=10.x with a clean dependency tree.

findings.title: node-gyp: Aggregated High-Severity Exposure via make-fetch-happen and tar Build Dependencies
findings.cwe_name: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')
findings.usage_in_codebase: node-gyp 8.4.1 is the native Node.js addon build tool invoked by sqlite3 during `npm install` to compile or download its C++ database bindings. It is not reachable at application runtime. node-gyp is the root aggregator of all six transitive vulnerability chains flagged by npm audit.
findings.description: node-gyp 8.4.1 is directly depended on by sqlite3 5.1.7 for native binary compilation. It pulls in both make-fetch-happen (carrying high-severity tar CVEs via cacache) and tar directly, making it the central aggregation point for the entire vulnerability cluster reported by npm audit. All vulnerabilities are install-time risks only.
findings.remediation: Upgrade sqlite3 to >=6.0.1, which requires and ships with node-gyp >=9.x, resolving all transitive vulnerabilities in this chain.

findings.title: sqlite3: Direct Dependency with Vulnerable Native Build Toolchain (node-gyp/tar/cacache chain)
findings.cwe_name: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')
findings.usage_in_codebase: sqlite3 is directly imported in app.js line 2: `const sqlite3 = require('sqlite3').verbose()`. An in-memory database is created on line 5 and a `users` table is seeded. The `/login` GET endpoint at lines 12-29 queries the database using `db.get()`. CRITICAL: The actual runtime risk from sqlite3 usage is not the build-chain CVEs — it is the SQL injection vulnerability in how the application constructs queries (see DEP-008). The build-chain CVEs are exploitable only at `npm install` time via a supply-chain attack.
findings.description: sqlite3 5.1.7 is the only direct database dependency and carries an aggregated high-severity npm audit advisory due to its native build toolchain (node-gyp 8.4.1 → tar 6.2.1, make-fetch-happen 9.1.0 → cacache 15.3.0 → tar, http-proxy-agent 4.0.1 → @tootallnate/once 1.1.2). The canonical fix is upgrading to sqlite3 6.0.1 which modernises the entire build chain. Additionally, the application's use of sqlite3 contains a critical SQL injection flaw (DEP-008) that is a far more immediate and exploitable risk.
findings.remediation: 1. Upgrade sqlite3 to >=6.0.1 (`npm install sqlite3@^6.0.1`) — semver major bump, review sqlite3 v6 migration notes for API changes. 2. Fix the SQL injection in app.js (see DEP-008). Both remediations are independently necessary.

findings.title: Critical SQL Injection via Unsanitized HTTP Query Parameters in /login Endpoint (app.js)
findings.cwe_name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')
findings.usage_in_codebase: In app.js lines 14-17, `req.query.username` and `req.query.password` (raw HTTP GET parameters) are directly interpolated into a SQL string with no sanitization: `const query = \`SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '${username}' AND password = '${password}'\``. This string is passed to `db.get(query, callback)` without parameterized placeholders. An attacker can trivially bypass authentication with: `GET /login?username=' OR '1'='1'--&password=x` or dump data with: `GET /login?username=' UNION SELECT 1,username,password FROM users--&password=x`.
findings.description: The /login endpoint constructs its SQL query via template-literal string interpolation of unvalidated query parameters. This is a textbook CWE-89 SQL injection. Any unauthenticated remote attacker can: (1) bypass authentication entirely with a trivial OR 1=1 payload, (2) enumerate all rows in the users table via UNION injection, (3) modify or drop tables via stacked queries if the driver permits. The database is in-memory so data persistence beyond the session is limited, but the admin credentials ('password123') are exposed on every bypass. This vulnerability is independently critical regardless of the npm audit findings and must be treated as the highest-priority remediation.
findings.remediation: Replace string interpolation with parameterized queries immediately: `db.get('SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = ? AND password = ?', [username, password], (err, row) => { ... })`. Additionally: (1) hash passwords with bcrypt rather than storing plaintext, (2) validate and sanitize all query parameters at the route level, (3) consider an ORM such as Knex.js or Sequelize to structurally prevent future injection flaws.

findings.title: express: Running on Maintenance-Mode 4.x Series — Express 5.x Available
findings.cwe_name: Use of Unmaintained Third Party Components
findings.usage_in_codebase: express is directly imported in app.js line 1: `const express = require('express')`. It initialises the HTTP server (`const app = express()`), registers the GET /login route, and starts the listener on port 3000. All inbound HTTP traffic flows through Express middleware.
findings.description: express 4.22.1 is on the 4.x maintenance track. Express 5.0 was released in 2024 and introduces improved async error propagation, removal of deprecated 4.x APIs, and updated security defaults. Express 4.x receives only critical security backports going forward. No active CVE is flagged against 4.22.1 at the time of this audit, but remaining on a maintenance-mode major version increases exposure window as the security patch cadence decreases.
findings.remediation: Plan migration to Express 5.x (`npm install express@^5`). Review the Express 5 migration guide for breaking changes (primarily around async error handling and removed 4.x deprecated methods). No urgent action required, but schedule within the next development cycle.

findings.title: Loose Semver Caret Ranges on Direct Dependencies — No Strict Lockfile Enforcement
findings.usage_in_codebase: package.json declares both direct dependencies with caret ranges: `"express": "^4.18.2"` and `"sqlite3": "^5.1.6"`. These allow npm to resolve any compatible minor/patch release. package-lock.json (lockfileVersion 3) is present and has resolved express to 4.22.1 and sqlite3 to 5.1.7. No .npmrc file exists to enforce lockfile strictness. CI/CD pipelines using `npm install` (rather than `npm ci`) will respect the lockfile but any environment without it will float to latest-compatible versions.
findings.description: Both direct dependencies use the `^` (caret) range specifier, permitting automatic minor and patch upgrades. While the package-lock.json v3 lockfile is present and pins resolved versions, the absence of a .npmrc with `save-exact=true` or explicit CI enforcement via `npm ci` means: (1) developers adding packages without `--save-exact` will introduce more loose ranges, (2) environments without the lockfile will silently install different versions that may be vulnerable. There are no postinstall scripts that introduce unexpected code execution beyond sqlite3's native binary download.
findings.remediation: (1) Use `npm ci` in all CI/CD pipelines to enforce exact lockfile resolution. (2) Consider adding `.npmrc` with `save-exact=true` to pin future dependency additions. (3) Commit and protect package-lock.json in version control. (4) For production deployments, evaluate pinning critical dependencies to exact versions (removing `^`).

outdated_packages: sqlite3@5.1.7 (major upgrade available: 6.0.1+; required to resolve all 7 npm audit CVEs)
dependency_overview.pinning_strategy: loose — both direct dependencies use caret (^) ranges, allowing minor and patch updates; package-lock.json v3 is present but no .npmrc enforcing npm ci or save-exact

summary.scope_notes: Full npm dependency audit of sqli-demo (Express + SQLite3, in-memory). All 7 npm_audit raw findings confirmed as installed packages. The high/low npm audit findings (DEP-001 through DEP-007) are entirely confined to the sqlite3 native build toolchain (node-gyp, tar, cacache, make-fetch-happen, http-proxy-agent, @tootallnate/once) — none of these packages are imported or reachable at application runtime; exploitation requires a supply-chain attack during npm install. The single most critical and immediately exploitable risk is DEP-008: a textbook SQL injection in app.js where req.query parameters are directly interpolated into SQL strings without parameterization. This vulnerability is unauthenticated, remotely exploitable with no prerequisites, and completely independent of the npm audit findings. Recommended remediation order: (1) fix SQL injection in app.js with parameterized queries (DEP-008), (2) upgrade sqlite3 to ^6.0.1 to resolve all 7 build-chain CVEs (DEP-001 to DEP-007), (3) plan Express 5.x migration (DEP-009), (4) enforce npm ci in CI/CD pipelines (DEP-010).
